coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are luminous, nearly spherical nanoparticles known for their water solubility, excellent biocompatibility, and adjustable chemical and physical characteristics, making them suitable for diverse applications. In this work, Gadolinium-doped carbon quantum dots (Gd-CQDs) were synthesized by hydrothermal method with gadolinium used as metal source, citric acid and melamine precursor used as a carbon and nitrogen source. The as-prepared Gd-CQDs were characterized by using UV–Vis, photoluminescence (PL), FTIR, XRD, HRTEM, and XPS analysis. The Gd-CQDs exhibited strong UV–Vis absorption peaks at 293 nm and 367 nm, with a photoluminescence (PL) emission at 526 nm and a fluorescence quantum yield of 19.7 % with an average size was 3.21 ± 0.3 nm. A novel dual-readout sensor, based on Gd-CQDs were also tested as colorimetric and PL sensors for the highly selective and sensitive detection of mercury ions (Hg2+). Upon interaction with Hg2+ ions, the sensing Gd-CQDs solution transitions from yellow to colorless is observable by the naked eye, while its PL remains quenched. The colorimetric and PL response to Hg2+ ions is good linear in the ranges between 0 and 50 μM, and the limit of detection (LOD) are 104.4 nM in case of colorimetry and 60.2 nM in case of PL. The reversibility of the sensor was also evaluated, revealing a retention of 90 % efficiency after 10 cycles. In biological assays, the Gd-CQDs exhibited negligible cytotoxicity, with cell viability exceeding 94 %, and were successfully used for bioimaging of Hg2+ ions in HCT-116 cells. Moreover, real water sample analysis demonstrated high accuracy in Hg2+ detection, with recovery rates ranging from 98.9 % to 103.8 % and relative standard deviations (RSD) between 0.36 % and 2.32 %. These results highlight the Gd-CQDs as a highly effective, stable, and selective probe for Hg2+ ions detection in environmental and biological applications.

Sonaimuthu Mohandoss: He received a doctoral degree from Alagappa University and has 10 years of research experience in Materials Chemistry, Supramolecular Chemistry, Organic Synthesis for Chemical Sensors, Computational Chemistry & Bioimaging Applications. He is the recipient of several awards including Basic Scientific Research-Junior Research Fellow (BSR-JRF) and extended Senior Research Fellow (BSR-SRF) at University Grant Commission (UGC), India (2011\u20132015). He received the award (Ministry of Science and Technology) MOST Fellowship for Post-Doctoral Fellow at the Department of Chemistry, National Sun Yat-Sen University, Taiwan (2017\u20132018). Moreover, he worked as a Post-Doctoral Researcher in School of Chemical Engineering, Yeungnam University, Republic of Korea (2018\u20132020). Currently, he is working as a Research Professor at the School of Chemical Engineering, Yeungnam University, Republic of Korea. His research interests are focused on organic materials and small molecules-derived carbon nanomaterials for Chemosensor, environmental, and live cell imaging.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are luminous, nearly spherical nanoparticles known for their water solubility, excellent biocompatibility, and adjustable chemical and physical characteristics, making them suitable for diverse applications. In this work, Gadolinium-doped carbon quantum dots (Gd-CQDs) were synthesized by hydrothermal method with gadolinium used as metal source, citric acid and melamine precursor used as a carbon and nitrogen source. The as-prepared Gd-CQDs were characterized by using UV–Vis, photoluminescence (PL), FTIR, XRD, HRTEM, and XPS analysis. The Gd-CQDs exhibited strong UV–Vis absorption peaks at 293 nm and 367 nm, with a photoluminescence (PL) emission at 526 nm and a fluorescence quantum yield of 19.7 % with an average size was 3.21 ± 0.3 nm. A novel dual-readout sensor, based on Gd-CQDs were also tested as colorimetric and PL sensors for the highly selective and sensitive detection of mercury ions (Hg2+). Upon interaction with Hg2+ ions, the sensing Gd-CQDs solution transitions from yellow to colorless is observable by the naked eye, while its PL remains quenched. The colorimetric and PL response to Hg2+ ions is good linear in the ranges between 0 and 50 μM, and the limit of detection (LOD) are 104.4 nM in case of colorimetry and 60.2 nM in case of PL. The reversibility of the sensor was also evaluated, revealing a retention of 90 % efficiency after 10 cycles. In biological assays, the Gd-CQDs exhibited negligible cytotoxicity, with cell viability exceeding 94 %, and were successfully used for bioimaging of Hg2+ ions in HCT-116 cells. Moreover, real water sample analysis demonstrated high accuracy in Hg2+ detection, with recovery rates ranging from 98.9 % to 103.8 % and relative standard deviations (RSD) between 0.36 % and 2.32 %. These results highlight the Gd-CQDs as a highly effective, stable, and selective probe for Hg2+ ions detection in environmental and biological applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The toxic heavy metal mercury (Hg2+) is non-biodegradable and poses a threat to the world soil and water. Hg2+ pollution can cause widespread health and environmental impact including kidney damage, neurological disorders, and development issues [1,2]. As consequently, mercury detection technologies that are quick, inexpensive, simple, and adaptable to complicated situations are required [3]. Therefore, it is important to investigate a simple, cost-effective, and highly selective analytical method for Hg2+ sensing. There are numerous methods for determining Hg2+ levels, including atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS) [4], inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) [5], and anodic stripping voltammetry [6]. However, these techniques need large-scale instruments, extensive preparation of samples, time-consuming procedures, and skilled staff, limiting their practical applications in standard detection of Hg2+. Consequently, researchers have sought alternative detection methods, with colorimetric and fluorescence-based techniques emerging as highly promising solutions due to their rapid response, high specificity, ease of use, and potential for real-time visual detection [7–11]. To date, many fluorescent probes have been developed employing materials such as organic fluorophores, quantum dots, and noble-metal nanoclusters [12–14]. However, their actual applications have been limited due to their limitations, which include hazardous ingredients, low photostability, low selectivity, and a difficult fabrication method. Thus, in order to improve the detection performance for practical use, new fluorescent probes need to be developed. Therefore, to enhance the performance and feasibility of fluorescent-based Hg2+ ions detection [15–22], the development of novel, high-performance fluorescent probes is necessary.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have attracted considerable interest due to their remarkable properties, including facile synthesis, tunable surface functionalities, strong resistance to photobleaching, high water solubility, and excellent biocompatibility [23]. CQDs with sizes less than 10 nm usually exhibit size-dependent fluorescence characteristics. CQDs photoluminescence can be ascribed to the existence of surface energy barriers that become emissive upon stability as a consequence of surface passivation. After years of development in food analysis, bioimaging, biosensing, theranostics, and electronic devices has broad application prospects. Hydrothermal treatment, microwave-assisted synthesis, and laser abrasion are three common synthesis processes, each with its own set of benefits and resulting in CQDs with unique features [24–27]. Among these, the hydrothermal process stands out for its simplicity, cost-effectiveness, and potential for large-scale production. CQDs doped with non-metal elements such as nitrogen, especially when combined with metal or rare earth co-dopants, can exhibit intense fluorescence, enhanced surface passivation, and strong resistance to radiation [25]. These modifications often lead to extremely high photoluminescence quantum yields due to the formation of excitation energy traps. The fluorescence behavior of heteroatom-doped or co-doped CQDs is primarily influenced by these energy traps and the resulting alterations in their electronic structure [28]. Research indicates that introducing metal ions can modify the electronic band structure through interactions such as chelation and coordination with functional groups on the CQD surface, thereby boosting their fluorescence efficiency [29]. Metal ions, with their readily available empty orbitals and ease of electron transfer, tend to interact efficiently with surface groups like carboxyl and amino groups on the CQDs. Therefore, metal doping is an excellent strategy for improving the fluorescence quantum yield and creating new optical property.

Gadolinium (Gd) is preferred because of its high paramagnetic characteristics, which considerably increase the relaxation rates of surrounding water protons and so improve the contrast of magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), fluorescence imaging (FL), and computed tomography imaging (CT) [30,31]. Furthermore, when chelated, Gd-based drugs are typically non-toxic, therefore free Gd ions do not cause harm. It is crucial to highlight that many different metal ions have strong magnetic characteristics; nonetheless, the primary issue in medical applications is safety [30,31]. However, free Gd3+ ions pose toxicity risks by interfering with calcium channels, leading to potential cardiovascular and neurological issues [32]. Unlike other metals with high magnetic characteristics, gadolinium has a low toxicity profile when chelated. As a result, the beneficial combination of CQDs and Gd is a promising strategy to developing dual-contrast agents that combine the optical imaging capabilities of CQDs with the increased MRI contrast afforded by Gd [33]. Moreover, these synergistic effect of nanomaterials offer exceptional water solubility, biocompatibility, and stability, making them ideal for multimodal imaging, targeted drug delivery, and environmental sensing applications [34–37]. Additionally, the integration of Gd into CQDs enhances their colorimetric and photoluminescence (PL) characteristics, significantly improving their suitability as advanced sensing and imaging probes. To the best of our knowledge, this study represents the first application of Gd-CQDs colorimetric and photoluminescent probes for the detection of Hg2+ ions in living cells and real samples.

In this study, Gd-doped CQDs through a simple hydrothermal method and used as a colorimetric and photoluminescent probe for detecting Hg2+ ions (Scheme 1 ). The synthesized Gd-CQDs probes were characterized using various analytical and spectroscopic techniques such as UV–Vis spectroscopy, PL analysis, FTIR, XRD, HRTEM, and XPS. These characterizations confirmed their uniform nanometer-scale size (3.21 ± 0.3 nm), excitation-independent PL emission at 526 nm (excitation at 367 nm), and a high quantum yield of 19.7 %. The presence of Hg2+ ions induced a visible color transition from yellow to colorless, along with PL quenching, allowing for both visual and quantitative detection. The detection limits were determined to be 104.4 nM for colorimetric analysis and 60.2 nM for photoluminescence-based detection. At the same time, in vitro cell experiments were performed to confirm the biocompatibility of Gd-CQDs with Hg2+ ions detection in biological system as confirmed via confocal microscopy using HCT-116 cells. Finally, the sensor also exhibited high accuracy in detecting mercury ions in real water samples, confirming its potential for practical environmental monitoring.

Melamine and citric acid was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (South Korea). Gadolinium chloride (GdCl3) was obtained from TCI Chemical Company. All of these chemicals were analytical reagents, which were used without additional purification. De-ionized water (DI water) was utilized in the entire experiment.

The green emission Gadolinium-doped carbon quantum dots (Gd-CQDs) were synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal method in the following steps: 0.5 g of melamine and 0.5 g of citric acid were dissolved in 50 mL DI water with the following addition of 0.1 g of GdCl3 under constant stirring. The mixture was then put in a 100 mL Teflon-lined hydrothermal reactor and heated to 160 °C for 8 h. After natural cooling, the reaction mixture is centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 15 min to remove larger particles, filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane, and optionally dialyzed using a 1 kDa membrane for further purification. The purified product was freeze-dried and stored at 4 °C for further characterizations.

In the colorimetric and photoluminescence on–off–on sensing experiments, a Gd-CQDs solution (5 mg/mL) was prepared in 3.0 mL of HEPES buffer (10 mM, pH 7.4). The final stock solution of Gd-CQDs was mixed with various metal cations (50 μM) and changes in the absorbance and PL intensity were recorded. To evaluate the selectivity of Gd-CQDs for Hg2+ ions, 25 μL aliquots of different metal ion solutions (50 μM) were mixed with 5 μL of the Gd-CQDs solution. HEPES buffer was then added to adjust the total volume to 2.0 mL at pH 7.4.

For the colorimetric and photoluminescence sensing using the Hg2+ ions concentration up to 50 μM were added Gd-CQDs solutions. The UV–Visible and Photoluminescence spectra were measures after 5 min of shaking under excitation and emission wavelength at 367 and 526 nm.

Water samples including as tap water, distilled water, and river water were obtained from the laboratory and the Yeungnam University campus and used for making the required samples. The water samples (3 mL) were spiked with Hg2+ values of 10, 30, and 50 μM. After equilibrium, Gd-CQDs (5 mg/mL) was added, and the tests were conducted for 1 min at the excitation wavelength of 367 nm. The water was filtered via a 0.22 μm membrane to eliminate any insoluble contaminants.

To investigate the optical properties of Gd-doped carbon quantum dots (Gd-CQDs), both their UV–Vis absorption and photoluminescence (PL) characteristics were analyzed. As shown in Fig. 1 a, Gd-CQDs exhibited distinct absorption peaks around 293 nm and 367 nm, which are attributed to the electronic transition from the ground state to the excited state (μ-μ* transition) of the aromatic sp2 domains, specifically involving C=O and C=N bonds [38]. The introduction of Gd dopants into the CQD structure creates surface defects within the lattice, generating new energy states that extend the absorption range into the visible spectrum. This modification is reflected in the visual appearance of the Gd-CQD aqueous solution, which is shown in the inset of Fig. 1a, where the solution appears yellow under natural light and emits a bright green PL when exposed to a 365 nm UV lamp.

The photoluminescence spectra of the Gd-CQDs revealed peak excitation and emission wavelengths at 367 nm and 526 nm, respectively, as shown in Fig. 1b. Using quinine sulfate as a reference standard, the fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of the Gd-CQDs was determined to be approximately 19.7 %. Additionally, as depicted in Fig. 1c, the emission wavelength of the Gd-CQDs remained consistent regardless of changes in the excitation wavelength, which ranged from 300 nm to 450 nm [38]. Further analysis in Fig. 1d shows that there was no significant shift in the PL emission wavelengths as the excitation wavelengths were varied.

To assess the stability of Gd-CQDs, we evaluated how various factors, such as pH levels, ionic strength, irradiation duration, and storage time, influence their photoluminescence (PL) intensity. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the Gd-CQDs exhibit stable PL over a broad pH range (from pH 3 to pH 12). However, a significant reduction in PL intensity was observed in highly acidic (pH < 2) or strongly basic (pH > 12) environments [25]. This pH-dependent behavior may be attributed to the protonation or deprotonation of functional groups such as –NH2, –OH, and –COOH, or to hydrolysis reactions involving −O=C–N–H bonds. At pH levels between 6 and 8, the photoluminescence intensity of Gd-CQDs remained stable, indicating good dispersion and minimal interaction effects under near-neutral conditions. The impact of ionic strength on the PL intensity of Gd-CQDs was also investigated by varying the concentration of NaCl from 0 to 1.0 M. As depicted in Fig. 2b, even at high salt concentrations (1.0 M NaCl), the PL intensity of the Gd-CQDs remained largely unaffected, demonstrating their excellent stability in saline environments. To evaluate the photostability of Gd-CQDs, we subjected them to continuous irradiation with 365 nm UV light. As shown in Fig. 2c, although there was a slight decrease in PL intensity with increased irradiation time, only a 5 % reduction in intensity was observed after 30 min of continuous UV exposure, indicating good photostability [25]. Further, the storage stability of Gd-CQDs was tested by keeping the samples under ambient conditions for up to 60 days. As seen in Fig. 2d, no significant changes in emission intensity were observed, and no precipitation formed, highlighting the long-term stability and shelf life of Gd-CQDs under normal storage conditions. These results confirm that Gd-CQDs exhibit excellent stability across a range of environmental conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural and morphological properties of Gd-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: FTIR analysis was conducted to examine the composition and functional groups of Gd-CQDs. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the FTIR spectrum reveals a broad absorption band at 3100–3500 cm−1, corresponding to O–H and N–H stretching vibrations, indicating hydroxyl and amide groups as primary passivating agents [39]. These groups enhance Gd-CQDs hydrophilicity and stability in aqueous systems. Bands at 2500–3150 cm−1 correspond to aromatic C–H stretching, while the 1600–1700 cm−1 region represents C=O stretching from carboxyl groups. The peak at 1714 cm−1 is attributed to amide (–CONH) linkages. Additionally, the absorption peak at approximately 1635 cm−1 corresponds to C=N stretching vibration, while the intense band at 1318 cm−1 is attributed to C–N stretching, confirming the presence of hydrophilic surface groups in Gd-CQDs [40]. The FTIR spectrum confirms the presence of abundant surface functional groups, while successful Gd doping was confirmed by XPS analysis.

XRD analysis was conducted to examine the crystalline structure and composition of the synthesized Gd-doped carbon quantum dots (Gd-CQDs), with the results presented in Fig. 3b. A prominent diffraction peak was observed at approximately 21.7°, which corresponds to the characteristic (002) plane of graphene. This suggests that the Gd-CQDs exhibit a graphene-like structure. Additionally, a weaker diffraction peak at 40.9° was detected, likely corresponding to the (100) plane of graphite, indicating that the Gd-CQDs possess structural characteristics similar to graphite [41]. Moreover, Gd-CQDs shape and size distribution were examined using TEM. The as-prepared Gd-CQDs (Fig. 3c) are well-monodispersed and have an essentially spherical morphology with a lattice spacing of 0.23 nm (inset Fig. 3c). The diameter of the Gd-CQDs particles was uniformly distributed around 1.5–6.0 nm with average particle size around 3.21 ± 0.3 nm (Fig. 3d). This suggests a relatively uniform size distribution of the Gd-CQDs, confirming their consistent structural properties.

The doping of Gd into CQDs was further confirmed by XPS. The XPS full spectrum of Gd-CQDs exhibited four main peaks at 145.9, 400.0, 284.5 and 532.0 eV binding energies, consistent to the elements Gd4d, N1s, C1s, and O1s respectively (Fig. S1). The elemental analysis confirmed the composition as 3.96 % gadolinium (Gd), 4.63 % nitrogen (N), 59.86 % carbon (C), and 31.55 % oxygen (O), indicating the Gd-CQDs precise elemental distribution [41]. The high-resolution X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (HR-XPS) spectrum of Gd 4d exhibited two distinct peaks at binding energies of 143.8 eV and 148.3 eV, corresponding to the Gd (4d5/2) and Gd (4d3/2) states [41], respectively (Fig. 4 a). These peaks indicate the presence of Gd3+ ions in the Gd-CQDs, and the results suggest that the coordination of Gd3+ and the chemical environment remain largely unchanged after the carbonization process. In the XPS spectra of carbon (C 1s), peaks were observed at 283.9 eV, 285.7 eV, and 288.4 eV, corresponding to the C–C/C=C, C–N/C–O, and C=O bonds, respectively, as seen in Fig. 4b [42]. The nitrogen (N 1s) spectrum, shown in Fig. 4c, exhibited two peaks at binding energies of 400.1 eV and 401.9 eV, which can be attributed to the N–C and N–H groups, respectively. Additionally, the oxygen (O 1 s) spectrum, depicted in Fig. 4d, revealed three characteristic peaks at binding energies of 530.1 eV, 531.4 eV, and 532.8 eV, corresponding to the C=O, C–OH, and C–O bonds, respectively. These XPS findings indicate that the Gd-CQDs possess a variety of polar functional groups, including hydroxyl, alkyl, and carboxyl groups [39,41,42], which contribute to their surface properties and potential for various applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric response of Gd-CQDs to Hg2+
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the colorimetric response results, UV–vis absorption spectral analysis was performed on Gd-CQDs in the presence of various competing metal ions (Fig. 5 a). Gd-CQDs exhibited a characteristic absorption band at 367 nm. Upon the introduction of different cations, a distinct response was observed specifically with Hg2+ ions, leading to the emergence of a largely red shifted absorption band centered at 391 nm [43]. This spectral shift was accompanied by a visible color change from yellow to colorless (Fig. 5b). No significant spectral changes were detected with other competing metal ions, demonstrating the high colorimetric selectivity of Gd-CQDs toward Hg2+ in an aqueous medium.

To further investigate the binding affinity of Gd-CQDs for Hg2+, titration experiments were conducted by gradually increasing the absorbance of Hg2+ concentration from 0 to 50 μM (Fig. 5c). As the Hg2+ concentration increased, the yellow solution gradually turned colorless, reinforcing the selective detection capability of Gd-CQDs. These results confirm that the detection of Hg2+ remains unaffected by the presence of coexisting metal ions. The calibration curve, plotted as 1/[A – A0] vs. 1/[Hg2+] (Fig. 5d), exhibited a strong linear correlation (R2 = 0.9942) within the range of 0–50 µM [44]. The LOD was calculated using the formula (LOD = K × SD/S) [44] and determined to be 104.4 nM. The UV–vis absorption response maintained a linear relationship with Hg2+ concentration across the 0–50 µM range (Fig. 5e), further confirming the reliability of Gd-CQDs as a selective probe for Hg2+ detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PL response of Gd-CQDs to Hg2+
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the sensitivity of Gd-CQDs towards various metal ions, their photoluminescence (PL) response was investigated. As shown in Fig. 6 a, the Gd-CQDs solution exhibited no visible emission under a 365 nm light source, indicating that its PL changes were not influenced by other metal ions except for Hg2+. Upon the addition of Hg2+, the solution underwent a distinct transition from green emission to colourless (Fig. 6b), demonstrating an “on-off” effect. This PL analysis confirmed the potential of Gd-CQDs as a selective chemosensor for distinguishing Hg2+ ions from other cations [45]. Under excitation at 367 nm (Fig. 6c), Gd-CQDs displayed a weak emission band cantered at 545 nm. The introduction of various metal ions (50 µM each) did not affect the PL intensity, whereas the addition of Hg2+ induced a significant quenching in emission, highlighting the system pronounced selectivity towards Hg2+. This selectivity was further validated through emission measurements.

To investigate the binding interaction between Gd-CQDs and Hg2+, a titration experiment was conducted (Fig. 6b). As increasing concentrations of Hg2+ were introduced, the emission intensity consistently decreased, reaching saturation at 50 µM. The observed color change from light green to colourless confirmed the PL quenching effect, accompanied by a red shift in the emission peak from 526 nm to 545 nm in the Gd-CQDs/Hg2+ complex. Fig. 6d illustrates the calibration curve obtained by plotting P/P0 values against Hg2+ concentrations, yielding a correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.9903 within the linear range of 0–50 µM [46]. The LOD was calculated using the formula (LOD = K × SD/S) and determined to be 60.2 nM. Additionally, the PL intensity showed a linear correlation with Hg2+ concentration within the range of 0–50 µM (Fig. 6e). The estimated detection limit is 60.2 nM, which is considerably lower than the values reported in previous studies (Table S1). The fluorescence quenching of Gd-CQDs by Hg2+ ions primarily occurs through photoinduced electron transfer (PET), where Hg2+ binds to surface functional groups (–COOH, –OH, –NH2), facilitating electron transfer from excited CQDs to Hg2+ and suppressing fluorescence. Additionally, static quenching arises from the formation of non-fluorescent Gd-CQD/Hg2+ complexes, and inner filter effect (IFE) may contribute by absorbing excitation/emission light. These combined interactions disrupt the emissive pathways of Gd-CQDs, leading to a strong “turn-off” fluorescence response, enabling selective and sensitive detection of Hg2+ ions.

Reversible sensing behavior is essential for both practical applications and a deeper understanding of sensor mechanisms by using EDTA. While previous studies have reported limited reversibility in systems responsive to Hg2+, this study highlights the Gd-CQDs system ability to maintain both UV–Visible absorption (transitioning from color to colorless) [47] and PL emission (switching from on to off) [48] reversibility shows Fig. 7 a and 7b. When Hg2+ and EDTA was sequentially added at a concentration of 50 μM and 100 μM to the Gd-CQDs solution, it was found that the efficiencies for recovery of UV–Visible absorption and PL quenching remained high, achieving up to 90 % retention after 10 cycles (Fig. 7c and d). These findings position Gd-CQDs as an effective, sensitive, and selective UV–Visible and PL-based sensor system for Hg2+ detection [48,49]. The system rapid response and reliable selectivity make it particularly suitable for applications requiring fast and accurate monitoring of Hg2+, while minimizing interference from other substances.

To assess the potential of Gd-CQDs for bioimaging, particularly in terms of their biocompatibility and performance in biological environments, a series of cellular studies was conducted using cancerous (HCT-116) and non-cancerous cells (NHAC-kn). Gd-CQDs potential (0–200 μg/mL) was investigated for imaging Hg2+ ions in living cells (Fig. 8 a and Fig. S2). First, a WST assay with a NHAC-kn and HCT-116 cell line was used to determine Gd-CQDs cytotoxicity. The cellular viability was estimated to be greater than 90 % and 88 % after 24 h, which indicates that Gd-CQDs (200 μg/mL) have less cytotoxicity [50]. The high cell viability suggests that Gd-CQDs are highly biocompatible, strengthening their potential for bioimaging studies [51].

Subsequent experiments proved Gd-CQDs ability to track Hg2+ ion levels in living cells using a fluorescence microscope with bright-field, fluorescence and merged images (Fig. 8b). To evaluate Gd-CQDs effectiveness in cellular imaging, HCT-116 cells were incubated with 200 μg/mL of Gd-CQDs at 37 °C for 15 min. When HCT-116 cells were incubated with Gd-CQDs (200 μg/mL), high fluorescence was observed (Fig. 8b) [52]. In contrast, Gd-CQDs-stained HCT-116 cells exposed to Hg2+ ions for 30 min at 37 °C display quenching fluorescence (Fig. 8b). A significant observation was the fluorescence quenching that occurred upon the introduction of Hg2+ ions, suggesting the formation of Gd-CQDs/Hg2+ complexes within the intracellular environment [53]. The findings indicate that sensor Gd-CQDs are cell membrane permeable, remain viable during the imaging tests, and can be utilized to image Hg2+ ions in living cells and potentially in vivo. The observed fluorescence response, combined with low cytotoxicity and high cellular uptake, positions Gd-CQDs as a promising tool for monitoring Hg2+ levels in live cell imaging and other biomedical applications.

A PL titration experiment was carried out to use Gd-CQDs to the quantitative determination of Hg2+ ions in real water samples such as tap water and lake water. Two water samples were spiked with different quantities of Hg2+ ions (10, 30, and 50 μM) and evaluated using the suggested approach. The results are summarized in Table 1 . The analytical data show that recoveries range from 98.9 % to 103.8 % across diverse water samples, and the relative error values (0.36–2.32 %) are less than 3 % in all cases. The content of Hg2+ ions detected by the suggested PL sensor was found to be in good agreement with that obtained by the usual detection method, showing the sensor feasibility. These findings indicate that Gd-CQDs provide a highly effective and reproducible method for monitoring mercury pollution in water systems.
In conclusion, Gd-doped carbon quantum dots (Gd-CQDs) with excellent optical properties and excellent water solubility were synthesized using a facile one-step hydrothermal process.

The as-prepared Gd-CQDs were characterized by using UV–Vis, photoluminescence (PL), FTIR, XRD, HRTEM, and XPS analysis. The Gd-CQDs results exhibited a high quantum yield of 19.7 % with an average size was 3.21 ± 0.3 nm, stable optical performance, favorable biocompatibility, and dispersibility in water. Gd-CQDs have excitation-independent PL properties in the 300–450 nm wavelength range. This study reveals the significant potential of metal and nonmetal co-doping techniques in altering the characteristics of CQDs. Interestingly, the colorimetric and photoluminescent Gd-CQDs sensors displayed remarkably Hg2+ ions selective yellow color and green photoluminescence switch over a wide range of tested metal ions and unaffected by the presence of the common coexistent ions. The proposed Gd-CQDs sensor is very much sensitive towards Hg2+ ions with a LOD of 104.4 nM in UV–Vis absorption and 60.2 nM in photoluminescence with a wide concentration 0–50 μM in PBS 7.4 aqueous solution. In addition, reversibility studies indicated that the Gd-CQDs sensor could retain up to 90 % efficiency after 10 cycles. Moreover, the successful recovery of Hg2+ ions detection in real water samples confirmed the practicality of Gd-CQDs. Finally, Gd-CQDs has a strong permeability and no cytotoxicity to living cells, so it can serve as an in vivo monitoring probe for intracellular Hg2+ ions. These results position Gd-CQDs as an effective and reliable tool for environmental monitoring and biological imaging applications.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Sonaimuthu Mohandoss: He received a doctoral degree from Alagappa University and has 10 years of research experience in Materials Chemistry, Supramolecular Chemistry, Organic Synthesis for Chemical Sensors, Computational Chemistry & Bioimaging Applications. He is the recipient of several awards including Basic Scientific Research-Junior Research Fellow (BSR-JRF) and extended Senior Research Fellow (BSR-SRF) at University Grant Commission (UGC), India (2011–2015). He received the award (Ministry of Science and Technology) MOST Fellowship for Post-Doctoral Fellow at the Department of Chemistry, National Sun Yat-Sen University, Taiwan (2017–2018). Moreover, he worked as a Post-Doctoral Researcher in School of Chemical Engineering, Yeungnam University, Republic of Korea (2018–2020). Currently, he is working as a Research Professor at the School of Chemical Engineering, Yeungnam University, Republic of Korea. His research interests are focused on organic materials and small molecules-derived carbon nanomaterials for Chemosensor, environmental, and live cell imaging.

Ramamoorthi Tamizhselvi: She received her MSc degree in 2017 from Thiruvalluvar University, India, and her PhD in 2024 from Vellore Institute of Technology, Vellore, India. Her research focuses on the design and synthesis of novel Schiff base molecules for applications as sensors, including the selective detection of metal ions, real-sample analysis, and biological studies.

Kuppu Sakthi Velu: He completed his Ph.D. in 2021 under the guidance of Dr. T. Stalin at the School of Chemical Sciences, Department of Industrial Chemistry, Alagappa University, Karaikudi-03, Tamil Nadu, and India. He then worked as a postdoctoral fellow at SSN College of Engineering, Anna University, Chennai, Tamil Nadu, India (2021-2022), and subsequently at Yeungnam University, South Korea (2022-2023). Currently, he serves as an International Research Professor (Assistant Professor) in the School of Chemical Engineering at Yeungnam University, South Korea. His research focuses on MXene-based heterostructured materials for applications in energy conversion, energy storage, and biomedical fields.

Ayyakannu ArumugamNapoleon: Dr. A.A.Napoleon is an avid researcher and Professor at VIT University, Vellore. He has completed his Ph.D. in 2015 under the guidance of Dr. F. Nawaz Khan, from Department of Chemistry, VIT University, Vellore on the topic of “Studies on synthesis, biological evaluations of few nitrogen and oxygen heterocycles (pyrazolines, isochromen-1- ones, xanthenediones). He has completed his master degree M.Pharm from Birla Institute of Technology in 1997 and Bachelor degree B.Pharm from The Tamilnadu Dr MGR Medical University, Chennai, India in 1994. He has published over 55 Research papers with good impact factor journal publications. His current research work is primarily focuses on the development of probe as Chemosensor for metal ion sensing applications, environmental, live cell imaging, and developing Carbon Nanomaterials. Also designing novel heterocyclic compounds and exploring for suitable pharmacological applications from synthetic medicinal chemistry and from natural products.

Mohammad Aslam: Dr. Mohammad Aslam, originally from Lucknow, India, obtained his Ph.D. in 2021 under the supervision of Prof. Yong Rok Lee at the School of Chemical Engineering, Yeungnam University, South Korea. He has since worked as a postdoctoral research fellow and is currently an International Research Professor at the same laboratory. His broad research interests include annulation and C-H activation reactions for the construction of heterocycles.

NaushadAhmad: Dr. Ahmad did his Ph.D from the prestigious Central University, Jamia Millia Islamia, New Delhi India in 2009 on the topic of “Synthesis of bridged bimetallic complexes: Characterization and physico-chemical studies”. He has completed his master and Bachelor degree from the Aligarh Muslim University, Aligarh, U.P. India. His current Research interest is Chemical sensors, Biosensors, Supercapacitors, H2 production, petroleum refining, Battery, Perovskites, Nanomaterials, Ferrites, Metal oxides, Graphene and heterogeneous systems. He has published more than 150 research articles in reputed International SCI Journals. Current Research interest: Synthesis of nanostructured materials (metal, metal oxides, carbon nanotubes, and graphene) and their applications as a sensor, supercapacitor, and in conversion reactions (DRM, SRM and alcohols).

SubramanianPalanisamy: He is a working as a Research Professor at the East Coast Life Sciences Institute, Gangneung-Wonju National University, Republic of Korea. He completed his M.Sc and Ph.D. in the Department of Animal Health and Management, Alagappa University, Karaikudi, Tamil Nadu, India. During his doctoral and pre-doctoral research, he worked on polysaccharides, nanomaterials, and their biological activities. His current research interests include the biological activities of natural polysaccharides, the development of polysaccharides conjugates for drug delivery, and the synthesis of green nanomaterials for biological applications.

SangGuanYou: He is currently a Professor in the Department of Marine Food Science and Technology, College of Life Science, Republic of Korea. He earned his Bachelor’s and Master’s degrees from Gangneung National University and Korea University in 1997 and 1999, respectively. He completed his Doctorate in Food Engineering, University of Manitoba, Canada, and later worked as a Senior Researcher at the University of Manitoba (2005). He is a member of the American Association of Cereal Chemist and serves as an editorial board member of Bioactive Carbohydrates and Dietary Fiber. His current research focuses on the structural analysis of polysaccharides, the preparation of polysaccharide conjugates for drug delivery, and their applications in pharmaceutical activities, including antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, anticancer, and immunostimulatory properties.

Seong-CheolKim: Prof. Seong-Cheol Kim is a faculty member at the School of Chemical Engineering, Yeungnam University, South Korea, specializing in Polymer Science and Engineering. He earned his Ph.D. in Polymer Science & Engineering from the University of Massachusetts Lowell (2001–2006) and an M.Eng. in Chemical Engineering from Seoul National University (1998–2000). His research focuses on advanced materials, including polymers, nanomaterials, and biomaterials, with applications in energy systems and renewable technologies. At Yeungnam University, he is part of a dynamic team advancing innovation through government-backed initiatives like the Brain Korea 21st Century programs, aimed at fostering emerging technologies in energy systems and green materials​.
